Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered throughout the course. I'd have good intentions at the start of each week, but things just never seemed to come together the way I planned. I'd usually end up doing most of my studying in bursts, often late at night when my place was quiet. With 11 online courses going on, I couldn't really give any single one the dedicated time it probably deserved. I relied a lot on listening to lectures while doing other things, which worked okay for me since I learn better by hearing stuff anyway.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch the course materials, then other weeks I'd cram in as much as I could when deadlines were breathing down my neck. I don't really have a set schedule that works for me—I'm more reactive than proactive, I guess. The regularity just wasn't there, and I've kind of accepted that's how I operate at this point.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline notification or someone in my peer group mentioning something that made me realize I needed to check in. Sometimes I'd get a message from a classmate asking about an assignment, and that would remind me to log in. I wasn't the type to just browse the LMS out of curiosity or habit. It was more like, something external had to push me there—an email alert, a deadline, or hearing about something I'd missed.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became something I checked automatically like social media or whatever. There were maybe a couple of weeks mid-semester where I was logging in more regularly because of a group project, but that faded once the project was done. The peer support stuff helped a bit because my study buddy would remind me to check things, but on my own, I just didn't develop that habit.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me because I can absorb information way better when I'm listening to someone explain it. I'd often have them playing while I was doing chores or commuting. The discussion boards were also helpful when people actually posted good questions or summaries—it saved me time trying to figure things out alone. Anything audio-based or that I could listen to passively worked well with how I learn.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials and PDFs most of the time. Reading long documents on a screen just doesn't work for me—my attention drifts and I end up re-reading the same paragraph multiple times. I also didn't engage much with the optional quizzes because they felt like extra work when I was already stretched thin. If something wasn't directly tied to my grade or didn't fit my learning style, it got pushed to the bottom of the list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was going to directly help me pass or if I could actually understand the material better from it. If I could listen to something and come away feeling like I got the concept, that was valuable. Also, if my peers were talking about it or if it seemed like something that would show up on the exam, I'd make time for it. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my success just didn't make the cut.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely different—like they were extras I'd get to if I had time, which I never really did. Required stuff had a deadline attached, so there was pressure to actually do it. Optional things felt more like suggestions, and with everything else going on, suggestions got ignored. I know that's probably not the best approach, but when you're juggling this many courses with limited resources, you have to prioritize what counts.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone when I wasn't near my laptop or didn't have good internet. I also used YouTube videos and other external resources when the course materials weren't clicking for me. Sometimes a different explanation from someone else made things clearer. My peer support partner would share notes with me too, which I'd study from without ever logging into the LMS.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was reviewing downloaded lectures or going over notes from my study buddy, and none of that would show up anywhere in the system. I'd be sitting on the bus listening to a lecture I'd saved, actually learning the material, but the LMS would just show I hadn't logged in for days. Or I'd be talking through concepts with a classmate in person, working through problems together—real engagement that's completely invisible online.
Q11: Yeah, that happened a lot. I'd download a lecture or save some materials, watch or listen to them once online to make sure they downloaded properly, and then never access them through the LMS again. I'd replay those files multiple times offline, especially before exams. The system probably showed I only accessed something once, but in reality, I might have listened to it three or four times on my own device.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engagement dropped off, usually when life stuff piled up. Money was tight, so I had to pick up extra work sometimes, which ate into my study time. There were also moments when I just felt disconnected from the material—like I'd fallen so far behind that catching up seemed pointless. Surprisingly, I didn't feel super stressed about it, but my motivation to push through was low during those times.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired from work or dealing with other responsibilities, the LMS was the first thing to get neglected. If I was confused about something in the course, I'd sometimes just avoid it rather than struggle through it alone. Having limited resources meant I couldn't always access things when I needed to—sometimes my internet was unreliable or I couldn't afford to sit in a café with wifi for hours. All of that affected how much I showed up online.
Q14: Oh yeah, there were times I'd log in, click through some stuff, maybe start a video, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be thinking about bills or what I needed to do the next day, and the material just wasn't sinking in. Technically I was 'active' in the system, but I wasn't actually learning anything meaningful. It was more like going through the motions to feel like I was doing something.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was just feeling overwhelmed by having so many courses at once. Part of it was that my resilience isn't great—when something felt hard or confusing, I'd push it off instead of tackling it head-on. I'd tell myself I'd get to it later, and then later would become the night before it was due. It's not a great pattern, but it's hard to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. Without a deadline, I'd let things slide indefinitely. When something was due soon, I'd suddenly become very active on the LMS, downloading materials, watching videos I'd been putting off, submitting work at the last minute. My engagement was almost entirely deadline-driven, which probably explains why my attendance was so low but my assignment completion was still decent.
Q17: Feedback did affect me when I actually received it and paid attention to it. If I got a good score, I'd feel a bit more motivated for the next thing. If the feedback was critical, I'd sometimes feel discouraged and pull back a bit. But honestly, I didn't always check feedback right away—sometimes I'd see my grade and move on without reading the comments. The peer support helped because my buddy would talk through feedback with me, which made it more real.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for someone like me. Good because I could work at my own pace when I needed to, bad because without strict structure, I'd drift. If the course had more mandatory check-ins or required participation at specific times, I probably would have engaged more consistently. The way it was set up kind of enabled my tendency to coast until deadlines.
Q19: The heavy reliance on reading materials made things harder for me since that's not how I learn best. When there were audio or video options, engagement was easier. Also, having so many resources available was overwhelming—I never knew what was essential versus optional, so I'd just pick things randomly or based on what seemed shortest. Clearer guidance on what to prioritize would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is confused or anxious and keeps checking the LMS looking for clarity but can't find it. Or maybe they're logging in, starting things, but getting distracted or overwhelmed before finishing. It could also be someone who's browsing out of guilt but not actually doing the work. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just spinning wheels.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. My LMS activity was pretty low, but I still passed because I was learning in other ways—listening to downloaded lectures, studying with my peer support buddy, using external resources. The system doesn't capture all the ways someone can engage with material. If you're efficient or you learn well through methods that aren't tracked, you can do okay without showing up much online.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked on, how long you spent on certain pages—the surface-level stuff. But they miss the quality of that engagement, whether you actually understood anything, or whether you were learning through other means entirely. They can't capture conversations with classmates, offline studying, or those moments where something finally clicks in your head. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to pass. It's about being mentally present when you're studying, asking questions when you're confused, and connecting what you're learning to something meaningful. You can be engaged without logging into a platform constantly, and you can log in constantly without being truly engaged. It's more about your mindset than your activity.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on my peer support buddy to stay connected to the course, or how much learning happened through our conversations. It wouldn't capture the times I was genuinely surprised by something I learned and got curious about it, even if I didn't follow up online. It also wouldn't show the financial and time constraints that shaped when and how I could engage. There's a whole context behind my numbers that the data just can't see.
